# #39 - powm returns wrong result? [Open]

> Username: atlascoder  
> Created at: 2018-01-12 22:49:01 UTC  
> Updated at: 2021-10-12 01:58:21 UTC  
> Url: https://github.com/boostorg/multiprecision/issues/39  

I faced an issue where `powm` function with the same argument returns different probably wrong result. Below are variables to reproduce.  
  
    base = -  
  
    exp =   
  
    mod =   
  
Java (all variables are BigInteger):  
  
    BigInteger x = base.modPow(exp, mod);  
  
    x:    
  
C++ (all variables are cpp_int):  
  
    using namespace boost::multiprecision;  
  
    cpp_int x = boost::multiprecision::powm(base, exp, mod);  
  
    x: -  
  
Python `pow(base,exp,mod)` returns the same as Java.  
  
Looks like a bug or I am using it in wrong way?

---

## Comment 1

> Username: atlascoder  
> Created at: 2018-01-13 17:19:04 UTC  
> Url: https://github.com/boostorg/multiprecision/issues/39#issuecomment-357451078  

A workaround was proposed here by user "sehe": https://stackoverflow.com/questions/48233368/boostmultiprecisionpowm-differs-from-bigintegermodpow-in-java .  
I am actually not sure if it is a bug or not. The author of the workaround says that it is implementation specific.  
  
I am not mathematician, so I can't judje about the rules, nevertheless, it seems a bit strange to me to have different results for the same thing from different implementations. From my point of view, it has sense to use at least distinguishing name.  
  
So, the issue can be closed if you see that everything is well.  
  
And many thanks for really lightweight and powerful library!

---

## Comment 2

> Username: jzmaddock  
> Created at: 2018-01-13 18:38:25 UTC  
> Url: https://github.com/boostorg/multiprecision/issues/39#issuecomment-357456620  

I think we're returning the correct result - or at least one that's consistent with the rest of C++.  
  
I need to look at the mpz_int case though, and better document this.

---

## Comment 3

> Username: LoganDark  
> Created at: 2018-10-27 20:52:06 UTC  
> Url: https://github.com/boostorg/multiprecision/issues/39#issuecomment-433654283  

> at least one that's consistent with the rest of C++  
  
Isn't the point of this library to be more precise than that? :>

---

## Comment 4

> Username: NAThompson  
> Created at: 2018-10-27 21:24:18 UTC  
> Url: https://github.com/boostorg/multiprecision/issues/39#issuecomment-433656309  

@LoganDark : I do enjoy the joke but just for the rest of the people who wind up here: The problem is not precision, the problem is non-uniqueness of [reduced residue systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduced_residue_system). There is no "wrong" choice of reduced residue system, so this is not a bug.

---

## Comment 5

> Username: dev-loop0  
> Created at: 2021-10-12 01:58:21 UTC  
> Url: https://github.com/boostorg/multiprecision/issues/39#issuecomment-940589198  

This is probably because Java and Python returns a positive number for a%b if a is negative, while Boost doesn't. Also notice how Java's x + Boost x = mod.
